title: Cristina
content:
I.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She should never have looked at me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If she meant I should not love her!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are plenty . . . men, you call such,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I suppose . . . she may discover
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All her soul to, if she pleases,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet leave much as she found them:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Iן¿½m not so, and she knew it
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When she fixed me, glancing round them,

II.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What? To fix me thus meant nothing?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I canן¿½t tell . . . thereן¿½s my weakness . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What her look said! no vile cant, sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About ן¿½need to strew the bleakness
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of some lone shore with its pearl-seed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That the sea feelsן¿½ no ן¿½strange yearning
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That such souls have, most to lavish
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Where thereן¿½s chance of least returning.ן¿½

III.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, weן¿½re sunk enough here, God knows!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But not quite so sunk that moments,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sure thoן¿½ seldom, are denied us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the spiritן¿½s true endowments
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stand out plainly from its false ones,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And apprise it if pursuing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or the right way or the wrong way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To its triumph or undoing.

IV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are flashes struck from midnights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are fire-flames noondays kindle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereby piled-up honours perish,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereby swollen ambitions dwindle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While just this or that poor impulse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which for once had play unstifled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seems the sole work of a life-time
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That away the rest have trifled.

V.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Doubt you if, in some such moment,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As she fixed me, she felt clearly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ages past the soul existed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here an age ן¿½tis resting merely,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hence fleets again for ages,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the true end, sole and single,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It stops here for is, this love-way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With some other soul to mingle?

VI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Else it loses what it lived for,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And eternally must lose it;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Better ends may be in prospect,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deeper blisses (if you choose it),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But this lifeן¿½s end and this love-bliss
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have been lost here. Doubt you whether
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This she felt as, looking at me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mine and her souls rushed together?

VII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, observe! Of course, next moment,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The worldן¿½s honours, in derision,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trampled out the light for ever:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never fear but thereן¿½s provision
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the devilן¿½s to quench knowledge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lest we walk the earth in rapture!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Making those who catch Godן¿½s secret
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just so much more prize their capture!

VIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such am I: the secretן¿½s mine now!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She has lost me, I have gained her;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her soulן¿½s mine: and thus, grown perfect,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shall pass my lifeן¿½s remainder.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Life will just hold out the proving
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Both our powers, alone and blended:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then, come next life quickly!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This worldן¿½s use will have been ended.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
